obit_text:
James Mark Williams was born in Montgomery, Ala., April 12, 1873. He entered the Military Academy in 1890 and graduated number five in his class, June 12, 1894. On the same date he was promoted to be Second Lieutenant, 1st Artillery.

He became First Lieutenant, 7th Artillery, March 2, 1899; Captain, Artillery Corps, July 1, 1901; Major, Coast Artillery Corps, Feb. 2, 1911; Lieut.-Colonel, Coast Artillery Corps, July 1, 1916; and Colonel, Coast Artillery Corps, Oct. 29, 1919. On June 29, 1920, he was retired as Colonel, U. S. A., at his own request and after thirty years' service.

After graduation he spent two years at David's Island, N. Y., and from 1896 to 1900 served as Instructor in the Department of Modern Languages at the Military Academy. During the next three years he was on Coast Artillery duty at Fort Williams, Maine, followed by two years of similar work at the Coast Defense Posts in and around Boston. In 1905 he went to Washington, D. C., for a year of duty as Assistant to the Chief of Artillery, War Department. From 1906 to 1908 he was on duty at Fort Totten, during which time he graduated from the School of Submarine Defense. For the next three years he served with the Coast Artilley at Savannah, Ga., and then for a year on similar duty at Fort Hamilton, N. Y., and Governor's Island, N. Y.

From 1912 to 1915 he was at Fort Monroe, Va., as Editor of the Journal of the United States Artillery and Librarian, Coast Artillery School. Going to the Philippines in 1915, he served for three years with the Coast Defenses of Manila and Subic Bays. Upon his return to the United States he commanded the Coast Defenses of Los Angeles, Calif., for a year and then, in 1919, went to France where he commanded Base Section No. 1, S. O. S., A. E. F., Montoir-en-Bretagne, for a brief period. Returning to the United States late in 1919, he served at Fort Totten, N. Y., until his retirement.

After leaving active service Col. Williams resided at Montgomery, Ala. While motoring from his home to visit his daughter at Fort Totten, N. Y., his motor was struck by a railroad train at Durham, N. C., Aug. 24, 1922, and he was instantly killed together with his wife, Mrs. Madelon Nelson Williams, and his sister, Miss Annie M. Williams.
author: SECRETARY, ASSOCIATION OF GRADUATES

obit_text:
Edgar B. Robertson was appointed to the U. S. Military Academy from Mass. in 1870 and graduated and promoted to be Second Lieutenant 9th Infantry, June 17, 1874.

He was promoted to First Lieutenant, 9th Infantry, June 15, 1881; to Captain, 9th Infantry, June 15, 1891; to Major of Infantry, 15th Infantry, Jan. 12, 1900; to Lieut.-Colonel, 9th Infantry July 31, 1903; and to Colonel, 9th Infantry, October 6, 1906. He retired from Active Service, October 25, 1907, for Disability in Line of Duty.

For twelve years after his graduation he served on frontier duty at various stations in the Northwest, taking part in the Big Horn Expedition, May 22 to October 27, 1876, and being engaged with Sioux Indians in the fight on Tongue River, Mont., the Battle on Rosebud River, Mont., and the Action at Slim Buttes, Dak. He then served in New Mexico and California for six years followed by a like length of service at Madison Barracks, N. Y.

In 1898 he went to Cuba with his regiment and took part with the 5th Army Corps in the Campaign against Santiago. After his death he was posthumously cited for gallantry in action at Santiago. He was made Major and Quartermaster, U. S. Volunteers, June 3, 1898, and after a short period of service in the office of the Chief Quartermaster, Havana, Cuba, he served as Constructing Quartermaster for a year at Bismark, N. D.

In 1900 he went to China and served there for two years, being for a time Provost Marshall, American Section, Pekin, and also for a time commanding the U. S. Legation Guard at Pekin. Returning to the United States by way of the Philippines, 1902, he served for three years at Fort Niagara, N. Y., and Madison Bks., N. Y., and then went back to the Philippines where he served two years until his retirement.

After leaving active service he made his home in San Francisco where he died, Aug. 1, 1924, at Letterman General Hospital, being survived by his widow and two daughters.
author: SECRETARY, ASSOCIATION OF GRADUATES

obit_text:
Major Benedict received his flying training in the early days of the Air Corps and served with that branch overseas during the Great War, only to meet death in his plane at Langley Field, Virginia, May 7th, 1925, while engaged in the performance of a tactical mission.

Major Benedict was born in Hastings, Nebraska, February 12, 1890, and entered the United States Military Academy in 1911. While at West Point he was an outstanding figure in athletics, especially football, and maintained a keen interest in sports to the time of his death. The story is told that during one of the Navy games in which he played (he had the distinction of helping to defeat the Navy three times) the ball was about to be put into play after a scrimmage, when it was noticed that Cadet Benedict had been left unconscious on the field.

Shortly after graduation in 1915, he transferred to the Aviation Section, Signal Corps, as the Air Corps was originally called, finishing his flying training at San Diego in the spring of 1917 and going overseas the following January.

His first command in France was the Cadet Detachment at Saint Maixent. At that time the Aviation Section, Signal Corps, was undergoing a tremendous expansion. Cadets in all stages of flying training, some not trained at all, were pouring into France and being assembled at Saint Maixent for assignment to various stations. It was the ability he displayed in the difficult task of handing these young men, so lately come from civil life to a foreign land, to war and the hazards and disappointments of training, that resulted in his being ordered to the Third Aviation Instruction Center at Issoudum, where similar conditions prevailed. During his station at this, the largest pilot school in the A. E. F., he was first in command of the Headquarters Detachment and later in charge of flying.

In August, 1918, when the A. E. F. was preparing for the Argonne offensive, Major Benedict received orders for the Zone of Advance. He had hoped to have command of a squadron at the front, but was sent instead to the Second Corps Aero School at Chatillon-sur-Seine, as Director, and, in November, promoted to the grade of Lieutenant Colonel.

Following the armistice he held successively the posts of commanding officer, Trier Airdrome, Germany; Air Service Commander, Third Corps; and Air Service Commander, Seventh Corps. The latter command took him to Neuwied, Germany, from which station he returned to the United States in August, 1919.

After a few weeks temporary duty in Washington he entered the Air Corps Engineering School, which had just been inaugurated at Dayton, Ohio, taking with him his bride, Miss Dorothy Potter of Chicago. Following graduation a year later he remained on duty with the Engineering Division at McCook (now Wright) Field until February 1922, when he proceeded to London as assistant military attache for aviation.

During his tour at McCook Field he narrowly escaped death while making a practice parachute jump. Owing to the spinning of his body as he leaped from the wing of the plane, the shroud lines of the chute became twisted and it failed to open. Noting that he was falling faster than another officer who had jumped simultaneously from the opposite wing of the plane, he looked up at his chute and was able to open it and the reserve chute he wore in time to avert a calamity.

In August, 1922, orders took him from London to the United States Military Academy for duty in the Department of Tactics. In addition to his duties as L Company's "tac" he lectured on Air Corps subjects and renewed the ever-pleasant memories of his foot-ball days by assisting on the coaching staff.

It was two years later that orders came assigning Major Benedict to the Air Corps Tactical School at Langley Field, Virginia, where, in the closing days of the school year, after completing many flying missions in various types of aircraft, he met his death while participating in an aerial attack on a captive balloon, falling on the airdrome in sight of his comrades. Air Corps officers, two of whom were classmates, cadets of L Company and a formation of airplanes from Mitchell Field escorted him to his last station.

Major Benedict was a graduate of the Center of Artillery Studies at Trier, Germany, the Air Corps Engineering School and the Air Corps Tactical School. He leaves behind him, besides his children Charles, Patricia and Calvert, the memory of a powerful personality, a nature clean, straightforward and courageous, without a trace of the pettiness that smudges so many of us. He liked or did not like, and that was all; a thing was right or wrong and that was all. He never quibbled, compromised or regretted. No doubt he would consider his own premature death as properly in line of duty and neither to be wondered at or grieved over. And yet he loved life. Active, athletic, keen, he would go from his work straight to some exercise, preferably out-of-doors, and return home at the day's end refreshed and buoyantly happy, bringing to a family who adored him, the best of himself and of life.
author: D. P. B.

obit_text:
Hoel S. Bishop was born in Fond du Lac, Wisconsin, February 11, 1850. He was appointed a cadet, from Fond du Lac, Wisconsin, to the Military Academy, West Point, N. Y., in 1869, and was graduated therefrom in the class of 1873.

He was commissioned as 2nd Lieutenant, 5th Cavalry, on June 13, 1873; he was promooted to 1st Lieutenant, 5th Cavalry, June 14, 1879; to Captain, 5th Cavalry, on December 12, 1892; to Major, 5th Cavalry, on July 8, 1902; to Lieutenant Colonel, 15th Cavalry, on February 26, 1909; and to Colonel of Cavalry, on March 3, 1911. He served in all grades from 2nd Lieutenant to Major in the 5th Cavalry, a period of more than 35 years, under such commanders as General Merritt, General Carr, General Wade and General Carpenter.

The first station Colonel Bishop was assigned to was Fort Whipple, Arizona, and upon reporting there, in 1873, he at once took the field against hostile Apache Indians and was almost continuously in the field until his regiment changed station to the Indian Territory, in July, 1875. He participated in the campaigns against the hostile Hualpais Apaches, the campaigns against the Apache Mohaves, and the Campaign against the Chimhueves Indians. He was in the engagements at Marble Canyon, New River, and at Mears Wells; and was twice recommended for Brevet 1st Lieutenant by General Crook, commanding the Department.

In the fall of 1875, to prevent trouble between the Indians and the buffalo-hide hunters, he was detailed in command of a detachment to accompany the Cheyenne and the Arapahoe Indians on their annual buffalo hunt through the western part of Indian Territory and the Panhandle of Texas. On his return from this trip he was sent in command of a detachment to pursue and chastise a marauding band of hostile Osage Indians. After a march of about 125 miles in 36 hours he overtook the Indians on Horse Creek, Indian Territory, dispersed them, and captured all of their horses, women and children. For this service he and his detachment were commended in a letter by the Department Commander, General Pope.

In 1876, Colonel Bishop took part in the Big Horn and Yellowstone expedition against the Sioux Indians, and participated in the action on Indian Creek, Wyoming, on July 17, 1876, and at Slim Buttes, Dakota, on September 9th and 10th, 1876. In July, 1877, he participated in the Nez Perce Indian Campaign, in 1878 in the campaign against the hostile Bannock Indians, and in 1879, after the hostile Ute Indians had killed Major Thornburg and had corralled his command on Milk Creek, Colorado, Colonel Bishop participated in the forced march, under General Merritt, to the relief of Major Thornburg's command, and also took part in the combat at Milk Creek and in pursuit of the Indians to Grand River, Colorado. He also participated in the Ute expedition during the winter of 1879-1880.

During the next ten years he served two brief details as recruiting officer at Jefferson Barracks, Mo., and the remainder of the time was stationed at various posts in Indian Territory, Nebraska, and Wyoming, engaged in guarding the border against threatened outbreaks of Indians, in protecting the cattle trail through Indian Territory from Texas to Kansas, in preventing the depredations of timber thieves and game hunters in Indian Territory, and in guarding the opening of the Indian Territory to settlement. In 1893 he commanded a troop of the 5th Cavalry at the World's Fair in Chicago, and then served in Texas until the outbreak of the Spanish-American War.

In 1898 he went with his regiment to Porto Rico where he served at Ciales Manati and Mayaguez, assisting in the formulation of the election laws, the registration of the legal voters, and the holding of elections preparatory to the formation of civil government in the Islands. After a year of duty at Fort Myer he went to the Philippines in 1901 where he served for a time in command of several troops of cavalry, some Philippine Scouts, and some Constabulary to prevent communications between the insurgents in northern Luzon and in southern Luzon, and where he also assisted in the formation of the civil government in the province of Rizal.

After returning to the United States in 1902, he was stationed in Arizona until 1907 when he was sent to the Army War College in Washington, D. C. From 1909 until his retirement from active service in 1913, he served with the 15th Cavalry at Fort Sheridan, Illinois. Subsequent to his retirement he lived in Washington, D. C. until his death at Walter Reed General Hospital on June 6, 1925. On June 9, 1925, he was buried in Arlington Cemetery.

Colonel Bishop was survived by his wife, two daughters, Miss Ann Upham Bishop, and Mrs. C. E. Coates, wife of Major Coates, Infantry, and two sons, John C. Bishop, and Lieutenant Hoel S. Bishop, Jr., Corps of Engineers.
author: SECRETARY, ASSOCIATION OF GRADUATES

obit_text:
James Warner Fletcher was born in Middlebury, Vermont, on January 16, 1900, the second son of Mr. and Mrs. John A. Fletcher, and the grandson of General James M. Warner, West Point, 1860, who commanded the 11th Vermont during the Civil War. After attending Phillips Andover Academy for a year, he entered Middlebury College in 1918. The next spring he was successful in obtaining an appointment to the Military Academy, which he entered in June, 1919.

During his four year at the Academy, he took an active part in the Corps functions, athletic, social, and military. One of his friends has said of him, "Everyone who knew him was his friend and he was the friend of whomever he knew. He was a gentleman, a scholar, and a good West-Pointer".

In 1923 he was graduated and entered the Service as a Second Lieutenant in the Air Corps. He successfully passed the course at Kelly Field, Texas, and was sent to Ellington Field for his advanced training. On June 29, 1925, while engaged in aerial machine gun practice, his plane dove into Trinity Bay, and he met his end. His body was recovered and borne to the home of his parents where it was interred with military honors, a troop of Cavalry from Fort Ethan Allen assisting.

At the time of his death, one of Warner's classmates wrote, "Always ready with a helping hand, of extraordinary ability, high character, and pleasing personality, he endeared himself in the hearts of his classmates and comrades in no small degree".
author: A CLASSMATE

obit_text:
Brigadier General Edward J. McClernand, Ret., U. S. Army, who died at Walter Reed Hospital, Washington, D. C., on February 9, 1926, was a distinguished son of a family long prominent in our military service. General McClernand was born at Jacksonville, Illinois, on December 29, 1848, a son of Major General John A. McClernand, who rendered distinguished service in command of the 13th Army corps during the Civil War. General McClernand entered West Point in 1866, and upon his graduation in 1870 was assigned to the celebrated 2nd U. S. Cavalry.

While in the Cavalry he served with great distinction during the numerous Indian campaigns of that period. He was brevetted First Lieutenant "for gallantry in pursuit of Indians and in actions against them at Bear Paw Mountains, Montana, on September 30, 1877", where he gallantly had attacked a band of hostile Indians and conducted the combat with excellent skill and boldness, though only a young Second Lieutenant. He later received a Congressional Medal of Honor "for most distinguished gallantry in action against Nez Perces Indians". He was, on February 27, 1890, brevetted as Captain for gallantry in the pursuit of Indians and action against them again at Bear Paw Mountains. In 1898, while in Cuba, he served as Adjutant General to General Shafter throughout the Santiago campaign, and was again brevetted "for gallantry in action against the Spanish forces at Santiago, Cuba, July 1, 1898", for which deed he was awarded the silver star citation. After the Santiago campaign, General McClernand served in the Philippines as Colonel of the 44th U. S. Infantry, Volunteers. In February, 1901, he was detailed as Assistant Adjutant General.

Throughout all of his early service he was continuously on active duty among hostile Indians. He was on frontier duty at Fort Ellis, Montana, escorting and guarding the surveying party for the Pacific Railroad, where he was engaged with Indians at Pryor's Fork and Little Rocky Landing, Montana. He acted as engineer officer of the Sioux Expedition, and of the District of Montana, being with the expedition that rescued the remnant of General Custer's command at the Little Big Horn, Montana. During his experience with the Nez Perces expedition he was engaged in several of the actions on Snake River. After further frontier service in the field at Fort Ellis and Fort Custer, he was, in 1879, ordered to West Point as Instructor in Tactics.

He was later appointed Aide-de-camp to General John Gibbon, with whom he served in Idaho, Arizona, New Mexico, and Kansas, and participated in operations against the Indian village of Orati, Arizona, in 1891; and he was in command of the expedition against horse thieves of the Navajo Indians in 1894. While with the 44th Infantry in the Philippines, he was in command at the assault on Sudlon Mountain, Cebu, in 1900.

When the General Staff was organized, in 1903, he was one of its original members. In 1905 he served as military attache with the Imperial Japanese Armies, both in Manchuria and in Japan. In 1906, he assumed command of the 1st Cavalry, at Fort Clark, Texas, and he later served at Rock Island, Illinois, and at the Presidio of San Francisco, California, where he was appointed Brigadier General and retired with that rank on December 10, 1912.

During the World War, General McClernand was recalled to active duty and given the important command of the post of the Presidio of San Francisco, where he served throughout the war. From that time until the time of his death, General McClernand resided in Washington, D. C., with his wife, who survives him.

General McClernand was buried with full military honors at Arlington Cemetery, Washington, D. C., many of his old-time associates and comrades being present to pay him their last honors. In his passing, the Army has lost a brilliant soldier of the old school and another one of those picturesque frontier characters who did so much to open the way in that part of the country.
author: SECRETARY, ASSOCIATION OF GRADUATES

obit_text:
Charles A. Williams was born at Wheeling, West Virginia, April 3, 1852, son of Francis Smith Williams, superintendent of schools of Wheeling, and Adelia Earle Williams. He was educated at the Linsley Institute, Wheeling, before entering West Point on September 1, 1870.

He was graduated and appointed second lieutenant 21st Infantry, June 17, 1874. His early service was in the Northwest, including station at Vancouver Barracks and Port Townsend, Washington; Fort Wrangel, Alaska, and Fort Lapwai, Idaho. During the Bannock Campaign he commanded a company of Indian Scouts and in the campaign against the Nez Perces was twice wounded. As a reward for gallantry in this action he was brevetted first lieutenant by Congress.

He was later transferred with his regiment to Fort Sidney, Nebraska, where he served on duty with his company and as regimental quartermaster on the staff of Col. Henry M. Morrow. He was on detached duty at Columbus Barracks, Ohio, on recruiting service for two years while the Twenty-first was in the West.

The next station to which he was sent was Fort Niagara, in 1890 and two years later he went to Plattsburg Barracks, which remained his regular station until after the Spanish War.

At the beginning of the War with Spain he went with his regiment to Cuba, being in command of Company A. He led his company over San Juan Hill, and according to War Department records, "under constant fire, the position taken by his company being the most advanced and exposed attained by the American forces in that engagement".

For gallantry displayed at the Battle of San Juan Hill, July 1, 1898, he was later awarded the Silver Star Citation.

Shortly after returning to Plattsburg, he received his majority and was assigned to the 17th Infantry, which he joined at Dagupan, Northern Luzon, Philippine Islands. During a tour of duty in this section of the islands he was for some time in command of the prison at San Isidro.

When the Seventeenth was ordered to the Southern Islands, Major Williams was appointed Governor of the Sulu Archipelago. Of one incident, illustrative of the character of the man, accurate mention was made editorially in The Nation, July 28, 1926, shortly after his death, in the following words:

"In Colonel Charles A. Williams the United States Army lost a soldier who belied the theory that all army officers must be militarists. A quarter of a century ago, as governor of the Sulu Archipelago in the Philippine tropics, he set the army an example which went unheeded. One Sunday a crazed Moro, sworn to attain Heaven by killing Christians, slipped inside the walls of Jolo, his barong hidden in his girdle. The shots, heard in the Moro village outside the walls, started a rumor that the Americans were beginning a holy war. In a few minutes a thousand mad Moros, knives in hand, were yelling at the gates. Colonel Williams did not order the machine guns on the walls to wipe them out. He bade his family goodbye, disregarded the assurances of his staff that Moros understood only force, and, unarmed, accompanied only by an interpreter, opened the great barred gates and had them barred behind him. He told the rabid Moro mob the cause of the shots, invited the chiefs to come inside with him and turned over the body to them for honorable burial. Life in Jolo continued peacefully. In those days Colonel Williams and his friends traveled freely about the island of Jolo. The later policy of force made it unsafe for Americans to travel outside the walled city".

Returning to the United States with the Seventeenth, Major Williams was for a short time again stationed at Vancouver Barracks. Promotion then took him to the Twenty-eighth infantry, in the Philippines. On this tour of duty he was especially selected to command the work of construction of the road from Illigan to Lake Lanao, one of the most important engineering feats accomplished by the American administration in the islands, and the first road to penetrate the interior of the island of Mindanao, second in size only to Luzon in the Philippine group.

This duty was followed by appointment to the Inspector General's Department, with station at St. Paul, Minnesota, as Inspector General of the Department of the Dakotas. He was on this duty when promoted to the grade of colonel and once more assigned to the Twenty-first Infantry which he commanded from 1904 to 1910, inclusive.

As regimental commander he served first at Fort Snelling, Minn., then at Camp Connell, Samar, P. I. Then he brought his regiment to Fort Logan, Colorado, for a three year tour, and then returned with it for a third tour of duty in the Philippines.

His last duty prior to his retirement was at the Army War College. After retirement he made his home in Washington where his death occurred after an hour's illness, July 4, 1926.

He is survived by his widow, Mrs. Laura Hall Williams, and by two sons, Maj. Charles Laurence Williams, Coast Artillery Corps, and Edgar Williams, a journalist. A third child, Constance, died in infancy.

A letter from Maj.-Gen. John L. Hines, chief of staff, dated July 16, 1926, closes with the following paragraph:

"Colonel Williams performed the many duties that fell to him in the several grades with efficiency and conscientiousness and on April 3, 1911, was placed on the retired list, at his own request, after having served more than forty years. He is remembered as an officer and a gentleman of the highest type by the large circle of those who were associated with him or served under his command during the long period of his connection with the Army".
author: EDGAR WILLIAMS

obit_text:
Lentilhon Wheeler was born August 5, 1898 in Washington, D. C., during the Spanish American War, the son of an army officer, Chas. H. Wheeler, a graduate of West Point, Class of '87, and Zella Lentilhon Wheeler whose home had been in New York City.

At the time Lentilhon was eight years old, his father was detailed to Manila, P. I., where Lentilhon spent two, very interesting years. He enjoyed life in the Islands immensely and it is probable that the impressions of those earlier years gave him the especial interest in foreign countries which later influenced his life. He also acquired at that time a familiarity with the Spanish language which was of material assistance later on.

General Wheeler was transferred in 1908 from the Philippines to the Watertown Arsenal near Boston, where Lentilhon's real boyhood days were spent. He attended private schools in Boston until he was prepared for high school and then attended and graduated from the Newton High School, at Newtonville, Mass. His summers during his high school days were spent in camps on different lakes in New Hampshire, with the exception of one year when he joined the U. S. Geological Survey in Michigan.

Although born in the army, Lentilhon showed little or no interest in military matters during his youth, as a matter of fact, evidencing a distaste for the army until he was induced by school friends to go to a student officer camp at Plattsburg. There he acquired an interest in the outdoor life and military activities and, as a result, for the first time exhibited a desire to attend West Point. At this time he was only sixteen years old, and therefore, too young to enter West Point. Awaiting his appointment, he entered Harvard University and while there had the misfortune of losing his mother, February 28, 1916, to whom he had been very close all his life. He had passed his Freshman year at Harvard before the Senator, who, as a congressman, had appointed General Wheeler to the Point, gave Lentilhon his appointment.

He prepared for the West Point examinations at a school in Washington, D. C. until he entered the Academy in June 1917. In the meantime, war broke out and his father was called to Washington previous to going abroad. At the Academy, although he limited his athletic activities to tennis, he was very popular with all of his classmates, but well-known to only a few.

Due to the policy of the War Department during the war, the original class of 1921 was graduated on November 1, 1918. After the armistice, his class was ordered back to West Point for a six months' post-graduate course. Upon graduation on June 12, 1919, he was assigned to the Coast Artillery Corps and accompanied his class on a tour of the European battlefields during July, August and September of 1919. On his return to the United States, he was assigned as a student to the basic course at Ft. Monroe, Va. After several months at Monroe, he decided to give up army life and resigned on February 28, 1920. From Monroe, he entered the sugar brokerage business in Chicago but the fascination of life in foreign countries was apparently too strong and, in November 1920, he went to Tampico, Mexico in the employ of the Standard Oil Company.

Two years later, he was sent to Maracaibo, Venezuela, by the Standard Oil Company of New Jersey, and filled various positions for the company during the next three years without a vacation. During this period, much of his time was spent in the jungles, several hundreds of miles from civilization where he was in charge of the camps, wildcatting for oil. His last station there was in the wilds of the Perija district, the home country of the savage Motilone Indians. To reach this camp from Maracaibo, it was necessary to travel the length of Lake Maracaibo, about sixty miles, by launch, canoe up the Santa Ana River some forty miles to a narrow-gauge railhead, built partly under his direction, and then ten miles by mule before the railroad was built. The trip required from two to four days. Frequently, Wheeler would be the only white man in camp for days at a stretch with fifty to one hundred native laborers working under his direction. At this time several uprisings of the Motilone Indians against the invasion of their country occurred and the camp, of which Wheeler was in charge, was in continual danger of attack. Sanitary conditions were by force poor and Lentilhon contracted a tropical fever necessitating a serious operation. By the time he had passed the convalescent period, the date had arrived for his first vacation in three years, but he postponed his departure for the States two months in order that he might be in New York for the Army-Navy football game.

Far away from relatives and friends, of whom he was very fond, hearing no English spoken for days at a time, he developed an ability to write and turned out many poems of considerable merit which led one to believe that he perhaps missed his real calling and might have done bigger things had he followed this line before.

He returned to the United States in October 1925 with an infection of the throat which proved incurable and the last few months of his life were spent at Saranac. Up to the date of his death, August 5th (his birthday), Lentilhon never believed that he was seriously ill and was still looking forward to life with a zest enjoyed by few.

He was survived by his father and one sister.
author: G. W. L.

obit_text:
Frederick T. VanLiew was appointed to the Military Academy from New York in 1877 and was graduated and promoted to Additional Second Lieutenant, 16th Infantry, June 11, 1881.

For the next seventeen years he served with the Infantry west of the Mississippi, except for a three year tour of duty, 1888-1891, as Professor of Military Science and Tactics at Michigan Military Academy, Orchard Lake, Mich. Most of his service was in Washington, Idaho, and Nebraska. With the Second Infantry he went to Cuba in 1898 and was in the Campaign against Santiago until August, 1898. He then spent a year on sick leave until August, 1899.

He was dismissed when a Captain of Infantry on Nov. 20, 1899, and from that time until his death was employed by the Equitable Life Assurance Society of New York. During the World War he gave up business temporarily and devoted his time to coaching candidates for commission, having classes at Columbia University and elsewhere, and also private pupils, and handling between 2500 and 3000 men, most of whom were commissioned, some with as high rank as Captain.
author: SECRETARY, ASSOCIATION OF GRADUATES

obit_text:
James C. Shofner, born at Mulberry Village, Tenn., Dec. 29, 1853, was appointed to the Military Academy from Tennessee in 1873 and graduated with the Class of 1877. Being promoted to Second Lieutenant, 21st Infantry, he served until 1881 in Oregon and Washington and then resigned from the Army.

He engaged in business in San Francisco and Portland and became a Brig.-General in the Oregon State Militia, serving from 1887 to 1891 as Adjutant General of the National Guard of Oregon. From 1894 to 1899 he was a clerk, U. S. Engineer Department, Portland, Ore., from 1899 to 1910 was in grocery business at that place, and from 1910 until his death resided at Sonoma, California where he operated a fruit ranch.

After an illness of sixteen months he died at Sonoma, Calif., November 22, 1926.
author: SECRETARY, ASSOCIATION OF GRADUATES

obit_text:
Thomas W. Winston was born in Illinois, July 15, 1865, the son of Captain Thomas Winston, Assistant Surgeon, 92nd Illinois Volunteers and Major and Surgeon, 149th Illinois Volunteers, in the Civil War. He entered the Military Academy from Illinois, July 1, 1886, and was graduated June 12, 1890.

Upon graduation he was appointed an Additional Second Lieutenant, 4th Artillery, became Second Lieutenant, 5th Artillery, Feb. 12, 1891, First Lieutenant, 1st Artillery, Feb. 10, 1898, Captain, Artillery Corps, Feb. 28, 1901, Major, Coast Artillery Corps, Jan. 21, 1908, and Lieut.-Colonel, Coast Artillery Corps, Sept. 21, 1913. He was retired for disability contracted in line of duty, July 7, 1914, but became a Colonel, U. S. A., Retired, March 8, 1920.

His first service was at Fort McPherson, Georgia, followed by five years of garrison duty at the Presidio of San Francisco and Alcatraz Island. After a year at Ft. Slocum, N. Y., he served for a year as Professor of Military Science and Tactics, Northwestern Military Academy, Highland Park, Ill. This was followed by garrison duty at Fort Morgan, Ala., Galveston, Texas, Ft. Slocum, N. Y., and Ft. Totten, N. Y., with a year at the Artillery School, Ft. Monroe, Va.

From 1903 to 1907 he served at Ft. H. G. Wright where he was District Artillery Engineer and Artillery District Ordnance Officer, District of New London.

From 1907 to 1912 he was on duty at Fort Monroe, Va., where he was Librarian of the Coast Artillery School and Editor of Artillery Notes and Memoranda and of the Journal, U. S. Artillery. He then served for a year at Fort Barrancas, followed by a year of service in the Philippines.

When the United States entered the World War he was recalled to active duty and served at the Headquarters, Central Department, Chicago, as Personnel Adjutant and Assistant to the Department Adjutant.

He died at Hemstin Hospital, Chicago, December 2, 1926, leaving a widow, Mrs. Alice A. Winston, residing at 1701 Ohio St., Lawrence, Kansas.

William Church Davis, a classmate writes of Winston as follows:

"The writer knew him intimately as classmate and roommate at West Point, and as messmate and close associate for years after in the service. At West Point he was president of his class and a senior cadet officer; and in the service a most capable, conscientious, and gallant officer, a graduate of the artillery school, of the school of submarine defense, and for many years editor and manager of the Artillery Journal. Genial and likeable in disposition, he was nevertheless a man of inflexible determination, absolutely unswerving in espousing and maintaining a high standard of temperance, morality, and performance of duty, loyally cooperating with his superiors and receiving from his subordinates that unfailing support which his high example inspired.

Always a man of delicate health, he was compelled to retire in 1914, but during the World War rendered valuable service in the adjutant general's office, Headquarters Central Department; since which time he had been connected with the staff of one of the military schools of the northwest. A host of friends both in and out of the service mourn his departing, and their heartfelt sympathy goes to his wife and children".

Milton F. Davis, 1890, writes of Winston,-

"Tom's standards never changed from those of his cadet days. Causes and reasons could never change his principles. To him right was right, and wrong was wrong, and that was all there was to it. Pleasure, or an easy way could never take Tom away from duty or the best way of doing a job.

Loyal to his superiors, unforgetting and true to his friends, devotion personified to his family,-no man ever had a whiter soul.

Tom Winston stood for everything for which a man should stand. A "Great Spirit" has passed by".
author: SECRETARY, ASSOCIATION OF GRADUATES

obit_text:
George Lawson Scott was born of pioneer parents at Lafayette, Oregon, Nov. 24, 1849, the son of James Scott and Margaret Payne. He died at Letterman General Hospital in San Francisco after a stroke of apoplexy, Dec. 5, 1926.

In 1870 Scott became attracted toward a military life and entered West Point June, 1871. After graduating in June, 1875, he was immediately assigned as 2nd Lieutenant to the 6th U. S. Cavalry, then serving in Arizona and New Mexico. He participated in the campaigns against the Apache Indians in those two territories where he spent a great part of his military life.

He left the Southwest with the 6th Cavalry to take part in the Sioux Campaign in Dakota in 1890-91, which was brought about by the Ghost, a Messiah dance excitement prevalent over all the plains of the West and North, as well as South, in the winter of 1890-91, after which he took station with his troop of the 6th Cavalry at Fort McKinney, Wyoming. When a large party of cattlemen were besieged near Fort McKinney by a party of cattle rustlers, he raised the siege with his troop.

Scott commanded the headquarters Guard of General John R. Brooke, Military Governor of Porto Rico in 1898. After the Spanish War he took charge of the Chippewa Indian Reservation at Cass Lake, Minneapolis, where his daughter, Mrs. Edward Marshall, now lives. He had charge for a while of the Apache Prisoners of War at Fort Sill, Oklahoma, in 1911.

He was retired at his own request after thirty years service and went to spend the remainder of his days in California.

He had charge at one time of Alcatraz Island in California and served again on active service during the World War at Fort McDowell, California. Col. Scott married about 1879 the step-daughter of Paymaster Maynadier at Fort Verde, Arizona, who died before he did. His only child is Mrs. Edward Marshall of Cass Lake. Besides his daughter he is survived by two sisters, two brothers and a sister-in-law, Mrs. Sophie Scott, 4127 Woodruff Avenue, Oakland, California. A sister, Mrs. Jessie M. Ellis, lives at 627 East 64th St., Portland, Oregon.

Col. Scott and I lived in the same room and tent together at West Point as Cadets for three years, and I rejoice to look back and remember that we never had a disagreement, not even the smallest tift, between us during those three years. He was a man of great good nature, scrupulous in carrying out his duty in all the relations of life, and he left behind him delightful memories wherever he served. When I went to hunt on the Mescalero Reservation in New Mexico in 1913 near Fort Stanton, where he had served long in his youth, I found the people of the countryside greatly disappointed to find that I was not their old friend, Lieut. George L. Scott, whom they had expected to welcome back to the scenes and friends of his youth, and I was delighted to find that his memory had been kept so green for so many years.

Although we loved each other and corresponded all our military lives and served for thirty years on the active list of the Army, it is a remarkable fact that I saw him only once after he graduated. Col. Scott made many friends wherever he went-his quaint speech, tinged with a dry humor, his upright character, his kind and generous nature endeared him to everybody, and I join with his surviving Classmates and many friends in mourning tenderly for his loss.
author: H. L. SCOTT

obit_text:
It was near the close of the strenuous period preceding the settlement of the great controversy between the states that Robert Lee Howze was born in Overton, Texas, August 22d, 1864. Nineteen years later, in the new era which had its dawn soon after his birth, we find him at the U. S. Military Academy at West Point, N. Y., laying the foundation of what was to be a brilliant career in national service.

When, as a recent graduate, Lieutenant Howze joined the 6th Cavalry in the fall of 1888, that regiment was stationed on the vanishing western frontier, in New Mexico. The buffalo had disappeared and the end of the Indians, as a savage race, was in sight. It was his fortune to participate in the final scenes of that great drama, first, as commander for several years, of a body of Indian scouts in government service, and then in campaign with his regiment against hostile Sioux Indians in South Dakota in the winter of 1890-1, including an engagement on the north bank of White River, near Little Grass Creek, on January 1st, 1891.

After this early demonstration of his qualities as a soldier and a leader, Lieutenant Howze alternately performed line and staff duties during the next seven years, and generally at important and interesting places, among them Chicago during the riots of 1894, and West Point, N. Y., in 1896, as Instructor in Tactics. By this time he was well known in the Army as an officer of force and enterprise, and soon after declaration of war with Spain he naturally received recognition by appointment as captain of volunteers and assignment as Adjutant General of the First Cavalry Brigade in the expedition for Santiago, Cuba. In this sphere of activity he added greatly to his reputation and acquired a host of new friends and admirers, among them Colonel Theodore Roosevelt of New York.

Upon termination of the Santiago campaign there followed duty for ten months as Instructor in Cavalry Tactics at West Point, N. Y. In the summer of 1899 he was busy organizing and training volunteers for service in the Philippines, and in October assumed command of the province of Ilocos Norte, which he held until end of November, 1900, as Lieut.-Colonel of Volunteers. For his able and energetic services against the insurgents of Northern Luzon under Gen. Tinio, resulting in the release of a number of prisoners, he was rewarded by commission as brigadier general of Volunteers, in June 1901.

During the next three years, to July 1904, Major Howze served with the Porto Rico Regiment of Infantry. He was Commandant of Cadets at the U. S. Military Academy from June, 1905, to February, 1909, and for three years thereafter commanded the Porto Rico Regiment of Infantry, to August 1, 1912.

This record, wonderful in variety and efficiency, was augmented during the five years before the World War by duty with his regiment at Fort Oglethorpe, Ga., and in the training camp at Winchester, Va., as well as in the suppression of the strike at Trinidad, Col.; by a course as student at the Army War College; and by service with the Punitive Expedition in Mexico in the spring and summer of 1916. His great interest in the Cavalry service and his sound judgment in professional matters wielded a powerful influence in the camp at Winchester, Va., for the preservation of the traditions and ideals of the American Cavalry.

During the first year of the war General Howze was on duty with the Cavalry Division at Fort Bliss, and later as Chief of Staff of the Northeastern Department at Boston, Mass. He entered the World War as Commanding General of the 38th Division, arriving in France in September, 1918, and participating in the Meuse-Argonne operation October 21-29, 1918. After the armistice he commanded the 3d Division on the historic march of the Third American Army to the Rhine, and during a service of ten months in the occupation of the Rhine Provinces, restored that famous division to the peak of efficiency and maintained its condition as among the outstanding organizations of the typical American Army on the Rhine.

After return to the United States in August, 1919, General Howze passed at once to the command of the El Paso district in Texas, and he remained on duty at that important station in various capacities, chiefly as Commanding General of the 1st Cavalry Division, until June, 1925. Owing to the conditions of unrest existing south of the Rio Grande at that time, the exercise of command at El Paso called for tact and good judgment, which General Howze possessed to an unusual degree. His careful forethought and preparation for any emergency, and the evident eagerness of his troops for the prompt execution of orders from superior authority, made an impression which prevented further disturbance of peace along the border. The favorable reputation he made in the community of El Paso will not be forgotten during the lifetime of the generation who witnessed his talented service.

The distinguished career of Major General Robert L. Howze of the Regular Army of the United States was completed on December 19th, 1926, when he relinquished command of the 5th Corps Area at Columbus, Ohio, and reported to the Commander-in-Chief beyond the Great Divide.

In recognition of his eminent services General Howze received the following awards from the American government:

1. The Congressional Medal of Honor "For gallantry in repulsing the attacks of hostile Sioux Indians on the north bank of White River near the mouth of Little Grass Creek, South Dakota, January 1, 1891".

2. Citation, with silver star for "For gallantry in action against Spanish forces at the battle of Santiago, July 1, 1898".

3. Citation, with silver star, "For gallantry in action in pursuit of superior forces of the enemy, under the insurgent General Tinio, in northern Luzon, P. I., December 4-18, 1899, through a most dangerous and difficult country, through hardships and exposure, thereby forcing the enemy to liberate twenty-two American prisoners held by him, December 18, 1899".

4. Distinguished Service Medal "For exceptionally meritorius and distinguished service. As commander of the 3d Division on its march to the Rhine, and during the occupation of the enemy territory, he proved himself energetic and capable, exhibiting superb qualities of leadership. He maintained an unusually high standard of efficiency in his unit, rendering eminently conspicuous services as a division commander".

From the French government he received the Croix de Guerre, with palm, and the Legion of Honor (officer).

Perhaps the most conspicuous periods of General Howze's service and the ones on which he would have loved best to dwell, were his command of the 3d Division for nine months on the Rhine, and his command of the El Paso district and the First Cavalry Division for more than five years, to June, 1925, on the Rio Grande. In both these positions requiring firmness and tact he achieved great success and distinction.

From the day of his entry into the service as a young officer, General Howze exhibited qualities of mind and body which attracted the attention of experienced soldiers and awakened the unbounded confidence of his superiors. He was a brave and most efficient officer, a just and kindly commander, and a charming comrade. Whether in the training camp at Winchester, in the occupation of the Rhine Provinces of Germany, or in command of the most important mobile force of the American Army on our southern frontier for a number of years, he was a loyal supporter and a capable and energetic assistant, as well as a devoted friend. His memory will be cherished as long as the units he commanded shall continue to exist, and his record and example will always be an inspiration to his comrades who continue to carry on in the service of our country.
author: J. T. DICKMAN

obit_text:
Col. Henry A. du Pont, of Winterthur, Delaware, (son of Henry du Pont and Louisa (Gerhard) du Pont), was born at Eleutherean Mills, Delaware, July 30, 1838. In the autumn of 1850 he was sent to the Rev. Dr. Lyon's boarding school at West Haverford, Penna., remaining there until early in 1855 when he entered the University of Pennsylvania. Receiving an appointment as cadet at the United States Military Academy, he left that University in 1856 and entered West Point, from which he graduated at the head of his class, May 6, 1861. He was appointed a brevet second lieutenant and then a lieutenant, Corps of Engineers.

His first service was in the defenses of Washington with Co. D., 5th Pennsylvania Volunteers, being promoted first lieutenant in May, and in the following July (1861) he served with his own regiment at Harrisburg, Penna. He was stationed at Fort Hamilton, N. Y., from April 19, 1862, until July 4, 1863, and was adjutant of the 5th U. S. Artillery from July, 1861, until May, 1864, and acting assistant adjutant general of the troops in New York Harbor from April, 1862, to July, 1863. On the 24th of March, 1864, he was promoted captain and commanded Light Battery B, 5th U. S. Artillery in the Valley of Virginia; was chief of artillery, Department of West Virginia, May 24, to July 28, 1864; chief of artillery of Crook's Corps (Army of West Virginia) July 28, 1864, and again chief of artillery of the Department of West Virginia from January 1, 1865, until the following autumn.

After commanding various military posts in Virginia, Colonel Du Pont was detached and ordered to assume the duties of post commander at Fort Monroe in that state, and upon being relieved in July, 1867, was thanked by Major General Schofield, commander of the First Military District, for his "efficient services". He was later post commander at Washington, D. C., and at Fort Adams, Newport, R. I.

During the Civil War Colonel Du Pont saw much active service, the battles in which he took a most conspicuous part being Newmarket, Piedmont, Lynchburg, Winchester (where his horse was shot under him), Fisher's Hill and Cedar Creek. He received the brevet of Major, U. S. A., September 19, 1864, for "gallant and meritorious conduct at the battles of Winchester and Fisher's Hill, Va.", of Lieutenant Colonel, U. S. A., October 19, 1864, for "distinguished services at the battle of Cedar Creek", and was awarded a Congressional Medal of Honor for "most distinguished gallantry" in the last named battle.

B. A. Colonna, who served on the Confederate side as a captain of cadets at the battle of Newmarket, has published a history of that battle in which he refers to Colonel Du Pont as follows: "Gallant commander, you have this day served your country well and your laurels are now the joint property of a united country". His military training and soldierly bearing at Cedar Creek were noticed particularly by Major H. F. Brewerton, U. S. A., who served under Colonel Du Pont and who in writing of that battle has stated:

"We were under a withering infantry and artillery fire at the time and as Colonel Du Pont was the only mounted man on this part of the field and was riding a gray horse which made him very conspicuous, I expected to see him fall at every instant-........ It was Colonel Du Pont's heroic bearing and distinguished gallantry at the critical moment, when infantry supports wavered and all seemed lost, which inspired the cannoneers and encouraged them to stubbornly hold their ground; and there is not the slightest doubt in my mind that but for his prompt and skillful dispositions and imperturable coolness and gallantry nearly the whole of Crook's Corps would have been captured by the enemy".

Captain William McKinley, later president of the United States, who also took part in the battle of Cedar Creek, has said that

"I had opportunity to observe Captain Du Pont in the campaign in the Shenandoah Valley and I found him always a splendid soldier, an officer of great courage and judgment, and who more than once distinguished himself in battle".

Colonel Du Pont resigned from the army March 1, 1875, and thereafter until his death was president of the Wilmington & Northern Railroad, being also its general manager from 1879 until 1899, when it was leased to the Philadelphia & Reading Railway Co. He also became one of the vice-presidents of E. I. du Pont de Nemours & Co. upon its incorporation in 1899, but in 1902 he retired from active business and devoted the greater portion of his time to agricultural and literary pursuits at his home at Winterthur, Delaware.

He refused the appointment as minister to Russia which was tendered him in 1897 by President McKinley, and in 1906 he was elected to the United States Senate from the State of Delaware, and reelected in 1911, serving until the expiration of his term of office, March 4, 1917. As a member of the Senate and of its committee on Military Affairs, of which he was chairman from May 1, 1911, until March 15, 1913, the Military Academy had in Colonel Du Pont a most loyal friend who held very dear the memories of his cadet life at West Point. He spoke at length in the Senate against the practice of hazing and brought about the insertion in the Military Academy Appropriation Act of April 19, 1910, of a provision to put an end to a then existing evil at that institution. He also secured the reinstatement of certain cadets who in 1911 had been sentenced to dismissal, one of whom later distinguished himself in the recent world war. But the keen sense of fairness which always animated him was shown by the great interest he took to right what he considered to be an act of injustice committed during the Civil War. In June, 1864, the large library, scientific apparatus and very extensive minerological collection of the Virginia Military Institute at Lexington, Va., as well as the quarters of its professors, were destroyed by order of General David Hunter, Colonel Du Pont being then chief of artillery of the General's command. Believing this to have been a wholly unnecessary destruction of private property and not justified by the rules of war, Colonel Du Pont introduced a bill in the Senate in April, 1913, to pay the Institute for the loss of its property, which bill became a law after the Colonel had appeared before the Senate Committee on Claims and gave very interesting testimony in support of the measure.

Colonel Du Pont's loyalty to the United States Military Academy never wavered, and as late as March 21, 1925, while in his eighty-seventh year, he was present at the West Point Graduates' Dinner held in Washington, D. C., and remarked in an address as follows:

"Instead of the battalion of four companies of which the corps of cadets was composed in our day, I understand that it is now expanded into a regimental organization. The large increase in the number of cadets which is implied by this fact should receive our strong approbation, on the express condition, however, that the authorities of the Military Academy continue to impress upon every cadet who enters that institution not only the supreme importance but the absolute necessity of striving to measure up as nearly as possible to the standards of former days-truthfulness, integrity, honor, courtesy and devotion to duty and to the interests of the government, the whole interwoven with a patriotic love and affection for our common country".

Colonel Du Pont is one of the authors of "Artillery Tactics, U. S. A., Assimilated to the Tactics of Infantry and Artillery", and of "Cavalry Tactics, U. S. A., Assimilated to the Tactics of Infantry and Artillery", (1875); "The Story of the Huguenots" (1920); "The Early Generations of the Du Pont and Allied Families" (2 vols. 1924); "The Campaign of 1864 in the Valley of Virginia and the Expedition to Lynchburg", (1925); and "Rear-Admiral Samuel Francis du Pont, U. S. Navy, a Biography", (1926), the last two works having been written in his eighty-eighth and eighty-ninth years, respectively-an eloquent proof of strong mental vigor that was unaffected by age. He has also written many pamphlets and articles on biographical and historical subjects, a great number of which have appeared in print.

Although a man of wealth, he was opposed to all ostentatious display, this feeling, no doubt, having been inherited from his ancestors, for in an address made at a dinner given to him by the younger men of the family in November, 1915, he spoke of having heard this question discussed in his youth by the older members of his family. "In the first place", said Colonel Du Pont, "they believed that the acquisition of money, if gained honestly and honorably, was advantageous and right, first because it enabled its possessors to enjoy the comforts and refinements of life; next because it afforded the means of bestowing upon one's children the best and broadest educational advantages; and last, and the most important of all, in their estimation, because of the opportunities thereby given to help and benefit other people. Their idea of doing this was not by putting one's name upon the subscription lists of charites, nor by leaving bequests in wills, but by direct acts of private benevolence within the reach of everyone, and which, though rarely if ever mentioned, called for infinitely more time and trouble; and they deemed these acts of benevolence much more effective because every dollar thus expended went directly to the person or persons whom it was intended to help. The old people, let me add, practiced what they preached; and while I shall not mention many examples that could be given, I may say that although more than one hundred and fifty years have elapsed since the death of Madame Samuel du Pont (Anne de Montchanin), the memory of her self-sacrificing charities and good works has come down to our own times; and that the life of my grandfather, Irenee du Pont, was signalized by countless acts of private benevolence, many of which were never known, and many more only discovered after his death. I will say further, in this connection, that the old people also believed most strongly and earnestly, that any personal assumptions based on the size of one's income, or any allusions in conversation or otherwise to the possession or expenditure of money, were odious and vulgar things". It is not surprising, then, that Colonel Du Pont declared: "We should not forget that it is very often easier to endure the strains of adversity than to withstand the snares and temptations of prosperity, which can be successfully met only by the exercise of much sober common sense, great self-restraint and thorough refinement of feeling".

Always friendly and unassuming in his manners, Colonel Du Pont was loved by everyone in the community in which he lived, while even those who differed with him in political and other matters, recognized his fairness and broadminded spirit in every situation. He died on the 31st of December, 1926, in his eighty-ninth year after a most useful and successful career, and the influence that military training, of which he was an ardent advocate, had upon his life, may be summed up in his own words written some time before his death as follows:

"As a young man I received a military training, as did my father before me, and, so far as I am concerned, have always considered that my entrance to the United States Military Academy at West Point was the turning point of my life, recognizing as I do that what little I have been able to accomplish in my time has been very largely influenced by the military regime of my early days".

Colonel Du Pont was married July 15, 1874, to Mary Pauline Foster, daughter of Herman Ten Eyck Foster, and their surviving children are Louise Evelina du Pont, who married Francis Boardman Crowninshield of Boston, Mass., and Henry Francis du Pont, who married Ruth Wales.
author: DAVID T. MARVEL

obit_text:
John P. Wisser was born in St. Louis, Mo., July 19, 1852, entered the Military Academy from that state in 1870, and was graduated number nine in the Class of 1874. Upon graduation he was commissioned as Second Lieutenant, Artillery, and served in that branch through all the grades up to and including that of colonel.

His first four years service as an officer included duty at Key West, Fla., Ft. Warren, Mass., Fort Monroe, Va., Washington Arsenal, D. C., and Fort Adams, R. I. From 1878 to 1882 he served at the U. S. Military Academy as Assistant Professor of Chemistry, Mineralogy, and Geology. He then served as Assistant Instructor at the Artillery School for Practice, Ft. Monroe, Va., until Jan., 1884, when he went to Europe where he remained until October of that year attending Schools and Colleges and witnessing the Autumn Maneuvers of the French Army. He then spent two years on the Pacific Coast serving at Fort Winfield Scott, Ft. Canby, and Vancouver Barracks, and acting for a portion of this time as Aide-de-camp to Gen. Gibbon.

From 1886 to 1894 he again served at the Military Academy as Principal Assistant Professor of Chemistry, Mineralogy, and Geology, following this with garrison duty at Ft. Hamilton, N. Y., Ft. Slocum, N. Y., and Fort Monroe, Va. While at Fort Monroe he served as Instructor, Department of Military Engineering, U. S. Artillery School, was for a time in charge of newly organized school for Electrician Sergeants, and was for a number of years Editor of the Journal of the U. S. Artillery. In 1901 he was the War Department representative at the Pan-American Exposition, Buffalo, N. Y. From 1902 to 1904 he served at Newport, Rhode Island, during which time he was detailed to witness U. S. Naval Maneuvers, Carribean Sea, 1902, served at the Army and Navy maneuvers in Narragansett Bay in 1902 and at Portland, Maine, in 1903, and acted as Inspector and Instructor for the National Guard of New Hampshire and Rhode Island in the summers of 1902 and 1903.

Going to the Pacific Coast in 1904 he served as Artillery Inspector, Pacific Division, and as Umpire in Camp of Instruction at American Lake, Wash., in that year. He was Inspector-General, Pacific Division, 1904 to 1906, including the trying times during and immediately following the great earthquake and fire in San Francisco. After serving as Inspector-General, Camp of Instruction at American Lake in 1906, he returned to Washington for a short period of duty in the office of the Chief of Staff and then went to Berlin, Germany in Nov., 1906, where he served as Military Attache until July, 1909.

Shortly after his return from Germany he went to Savannah, Ga., where he commanded the Artillery District until 1911. In the latter year he went again to the Pacific Coast where he served until 1913, being in command at various times of the Presidio of San Francisco, Calif., Fort Winfield Scott, Calif., the Department of California, and the Pacific Coast Artillery District. On May 26, 1913, he was appointed a Brigadier-General, U. S. A., and sent to Texas City, Texas, where he commanded the 4th Brigade, 2nd Division until January of 1914. He next commanded the Pacific Coast Artillery District for a year and then went to Hawaii where he commanded Schofield Barracks and the 1st Hawaiian Brigade from Jan. to Nov., 1915, and the Hawaiian Department from Nov., 1915, to May, 1916. On July 19, 1916, he was retired from active service as having reached sixty-four years of age.

During the World War he was recalled to active duty and commanded the Hawaiian Department from Sept., 1917, until May, 1918.

Few officers in the service have been privileged to contribute to the education and moulding of the personnel of the Army to the extent exerted by General Wisser, considering that he served twelve years as Assistant Professor of Chemistry at the Military Academy, served as instructor in the Artillery School at Fort Monroe, and was for about seven years editor of the Journal of the U. S. Artillery. In addition to these professional services so ably performed, he also found time to contribute many articles on military subjects for American and foreign military publicatons, for Johnson's New Universal Cyclopaedia, and for the National Cyclopaedia of American Biography. Among his articles may be mentioned, "Staff and Staff Schools", "The Second Boer War", and "A Military and Naval Dictionary".

He was a member of numerous clubs, service and social, including the University, Century, Army and Navy of Washington, Old Freibergers (London), and the Bohemian and Faculty of San Francisco. At the time of his death, and for several years prior thereto, he was president of the Retired Officers Association. With all who came in contact with him, he was most popular because of his lovable disposition and genial personality.

He died at Letterman Hospital, San Francisco, Jan. 19, 1927, survived by his widow, Georgiana Wisser, and two sons, Edward Hollister Wisser and John Philip Wisser, Jr., residing at 1986 Yosemite Road, Berkeley, Calif.

On Feb. 18, 1927, the San Francisco Branch of the Retired Officers Association adopted the following resolution,-

"Resolved: That in the passing of Brig.-Gen. John P. Wisser, U. S. A., retired, at the Letterman General Hospital on January 19, 1927, this association has lost a brave comrade, an esteemed friend, and an able, just, and efficient presiding officer. He was most prompt in attendance, generous in his counsel, and unerring in his rulings. He would often discommode himself by his regular attendance at our meetings during the past four years that we might obtain equality of treatment for all retired officers regardless of date of retirement, which end was finally obtained through congressional legislation, notwithstanding the fact there was no gain to be derived by himself other than the pleasure one receives from serving his friends and his brother officers. He was a true disciple of Mars, an able exponent of the military exploits of his day, congenial in his companionships, a good neighbor, an exceptionally loyal citizen, and a most devoted member of his family.

We will miss the inspiration of his presence, but feel that our lives have been made richer and deeper because of the sacred memories which association with such a character engenders".
author: SECRETARY, ASSOCIATION OF GRADUATES

obit_text:
Edward H. Plummer was born at Elkridge, Howard County, Maryland, Sept. 24, 1855, and was appointed to the Military Academy from Maryland in 1873. He was graduated June 14, 1877, and commissioned as Second Lieutenant, 10th Infantry.

He was promoted to First Lieutenant, 10th Infantry, Nov. 29, 1884; to Captain, 10th Infantry, April 26, 1898; to Major, 3d Infantry, Dec. 31, 1902; to Lieut.-Colonel, 28th Infantry, Oct. 20, 1910; to Colonel, 28th Infantry, March 11, 1911; and to Brig.-General, U. S. Army, March 14, 1918. He was commissioned Lieut.-Colonel, U. S. Volunteers, July 5, 1899; Colonel, 35th Infantry, U. S. Volunteers, Dec. 16, 1899; Brig.-General, N. A., July 1, 1916, and Major-General, N. A., Aug. 5, 1917.

Upon graduating from the Military Academy he spent two years on frontier duty in Texas and then five years at Ft. Mackinac, Michigan. Returning to the southwest in 1884, he served at various stations in New Mexico and Oklahoma, acting for over a year as Agent for the Navajo and Moqui Indians and at another time commanding a company of Apache Indians. With the exception of two years spent on recruiting duty at Buffalo, N. Y., duty in the southwest occupied the years up to the outbreak of the Spanish-American War.

In 1898 he went to Mobile, Ala., and Tampa, Fla., with his regiment and thence to Cuba, serving for a short time as Quartermaster at Headquarters 5th Army Corps, in camp near Santiago, and then as Aide-de-camp to Major-General Shafter, returning to the United States by way of Camp Wikoff, and thence to the Pacific Coast. In the summer of 1899 he organized the 35th Infantry, U. S. Volunteers, at Vancouver Barracks, and also some additional volunteer troops, sailing with the 35th Inf., Volunteers, from Portland, Ore., for the Philippines in October, and being placed in command of the regiment shortly after.

In the Phillipines he commanded his regiment under General Lawton at the capture of San Miguel de Mayumo and at various other places, operated under General Funston, Aug. 3 to Sept. 2, 1900, and performed various other military duties until the departure of his regiment for the United States in March, 1901. Being mustered out of the volunteer service with his regiment, he served for a short time as Aide-de-camp to General Shafter, then as Recruiting Officer, San Francisco, next for a year as Quartermaster, Discharge and Detention Camp, Angel Island, and then for a year as Constructing Quartermaster at Monterey.

From 1904 to 1906 he commanded Fort Egbert, Alaska, and then served at Fort Wright, Wash., until 1909, when he was sent to the Philippines for a brief tour, returning to the United States in 1910 to serve with the 28th Infantry at Fort Snelling, Minn., and San Antonio, Texas, until 1913. He went to Vera Cruz, Mexico, with the American Expeditionary Force in 1914, and acted there as Provost Marshall for Vera Cruz and adjacent territory occupied by the United States. Upon his return to the United States in the fall of 1914, he commanded his regiment at Galveston, Texas, and it was during this period that the troops went through the great Texas Coast storm of 1915. After commanding at various other frontier posts in Texas he was placed in command of the District and 16th Provisional Division at Nogales, Arizona, in 1916, and in 1917 was sent for a short time to command the troops in the Canal Zone at Panama.

In the summer of 1917 he organized the 88th Division, National Army, at Camp Dodge, Des Moines, Iowa, and remained with it until Feb., 1918, except for a tour of inspection in France during the winter when he visited the British, French, and American fronts. From February to October, 1918, he commanded the post of Fort Sill, Okla., and the various schools of instruction held there. He commanded Camp Grant, Rockford, Ill., from October 18th to November 30th, 1918, and on the latter date retired at his own request after over forty years of service.

Edward H. Plummer was an able, conscientious, and gallant officer as is attested by the many highly commendatory reports officially made upon him and upon his work at various times during his military career. In 1898 he was recommended for Brevet Major for gallantry at Santiago, Cuba, July 2, 1898, and also for faithful and meritorious services throughout the Santiago Campaign. In 1901 General F. D. Grant recommended him for appointment as a brigadier-general, U. S. Army, because of his executive ability as well as for his great military ability in handling troops and for his valuable services in bringing about peace in Northern Luzon.

In 1915 General George Bell, Commanding the 5th Brigade at Galveston, reported.-

"His ability as regimental commander is evidenced by the esprit de corps of his regiment, by the sanitary condition of his camp, by the excellence of his regiment in drill and maneuver ........ An order given to him is not only executed well, but as if it were a pleasure ......... He is a man of the highest character, great energy, and good judgment. I recommend his promotion to the grade of Brigadier General when a vacancy occurs".

The same year General J. F. Bell said of him,-

"Colonel Plummer has been under my observation for about a year ........ I do not hesitate to say without reservation that he is the best Regimental Commander I have ever known in my forty years of service. His character is irreproachable. In efficiency and every desirable quality his regiment is far superior to any other in this division. It is in a class by itself........he is more worthy of advancement than any other colonel I know in the service. I feel certain that his advancement would meet with universal approval".

General Plummer died at Pacific Grove, California, February 11, 1927, being survived by his widow, Mrs. Georgia Moody Plummer with residence at 872 Laurel Ave., Pacific Grove, California.
author: SECRETARY, ASSOCIATION OF GRADUATES

obit_text:
Charles DeLano Hine was born at Vienna, Fairfax County, Virginia, March 15th, 1867. He was the son of Major Orrin Eugene and Alma (DeLano) Hine, both of whom were of New England ancestry.

The boy was brought up on his father's farm, and after passing through the Washington, D. C. High School, entered the employ of a contractor. He won his appointment to the Military Academy in a competitive examination, and graduated in June, 1891, being commissoned Second Lieutenant of the 6th Infantry.

His original station was with Regimental Headquarters at Fort Thomas, but on his own request he was transferred to a single company at Newport Barracks, in order that he might attend the Law School in Cincinnati, just across the River. He graduated at the Cincinnati Law School in 1893, and was admitted to the bar in the States of Kentucky and Virginia, and in the District of Columbia.

Hine had always turned towards railroading, which fact caused him to resign from the service in 1895, and enter the employ of the Big Four Railroad as a brakeman. From this beginning, he passed through the different departments of practical railroading, working as freight brakeman, switchman, yardmaster, emergency conductor, chief clerk, train master, assistant superintendent, right of way agent, general superintendent, receiver, general manager, and Vice-President, besides holding various unique staff positions in the Railway service while doing special staff work of different kinds.

At the outbreak of the Spanish War in '98, Hine was chief clerk to the Superintendent of the Cleveland Division of the Big Four, which position he resigned to enter the Volunteers as a Major with the 2nd Battalion of the 1st District of Columbia Regiment. He served with this Regiment at Camp Alger, Virginia, Chickamauga Park, Georgia, Tampa, Florida, in the siege and occupation of Santiago, Cuba, and at Montauk Point, New York. He was honorably discharged from the service November 20th, 1898.

He resumed work with the Big Four the first of January, '99, and shortly became train master of the Chicago Division at Cincinnati, resigning this position to go as General Superintendent of the Findlay, Fort Wayne & Western Railroad.

About this time, Hine's father died, and the same sense of loyalty which had impelled him to respond to his country's need caused him now to surrender the position he had attained in the Railroad world in order to be with his mother and sister in the settlement and development of his father's estate. For four years, from 1899, he was engaged in agriculture and the real estate business at Vienna, Virginia. When he had completed his work there, he again went back into railroading, this time with the Interstate Commerce Commission.

In 1907, he went into the service of the Interior Department in revising its business methods, and in 1910, as special representative of President Taft, he outlined a program for improving organization and methods of the Executive Departments of the Government at Washington. During a portion of this time, he developed the idea of applying the system of inspection in the Army to the operating departments of railways, and in this capacity helped to reorganize the operating departments of a number of the trunk lines of the United States.

He was the originator of the unit system for railways, which was introduced into all railways of the Harriman lines while he was acting as organization expert with the Union Pacific System-Southern Pacific Company, during the years from 1908 until 1911.

In 1912 and '13, he was Vice-President and General Manager of the Southern Pacific line in Mexico, and the Arizona Eastern at Tuscon, Arizona, and had numerous encounters with the nomadic forces of Mexican rebels in the revolutionary zones of Mexico.

During his period of railway service, Hine became a writer of note on railway subjects. His artcles were published, as a rule, in the various railway magazines of the country, in which also appeared his most ambitious literary effort, under the title of "Letters From an Old Railroad Official to His Son". This series was likewise published in book form.

His ability as a railway operative expert elicited the statement from America's railway wizard, the late E. H. Harriman, that "He was the best expert in railway organization and efficiency in the United States".

In 1915 Hine married Helen, the daughter of the late John C. Underwood of Covington, Kentucky, a Confederate veteran and formerly Lieutenant Governor of Kentucky.

When the United States entered the World War, Hine again gave up his established and highly recognized work with the railroads to re-enter the service of his country. He was commissioned Colonel of Infantry and assigned command of the famous 69th New York, which with detachments from the 7th, 12th, 14th, and 23rd Regiments of the National Guard of New York, entered the Federal Service as the 165th Regiment. With this organization, Colonel Hine went into training at Camp Mills, Hempstead, Long Island, and later went to France in command of the Regiment in the 42nd or Rainbow Division.

After some months in command of his Regiment in France, Colonel Hine was relieved. This incident nearly broke his heart, and is referred to only for the reason that it furnishes the background for one of the spectacular incidents of his life.

As to just what causes brought about his relief from command, it is not necessary in this paper to enter. The writer does not desire to engage in any controversial discussion as to these causes, but simply wants to say in explanation of the episode which formed the darkest hour in his friend's life that in conversation with the officer who succeeded Colonel Hine, Colonel John W. Barker, now deceased, Colonel Barker said that he found the spirit and discipline of the Regiment of high order, and such deficiencies as existed were not the fault of the Colonel, but the result of conditions beyond his control.

Subsequent to his service with the 169 Regiment, Colonel Hine attended and was graduated from the Staff College at Langres, France. As an observer, he was sent to St. Mihiel, and attached himself to his old Regiment in the battle of that name. Without command or position of any kind, he went over the top at St. Mihiel with the first wave of the men of his old Regiment, and well in advance of its lines, the snow white head of their old commanding officer was the plume that beckoned them forward. This action on Hine's part was beyond the highest call of duty in the position which he at that time occupied, and was unquestionably done to show the men of his old command that in the crucial test of battle, which was the objective of all the rigors of discipline and training to which he had subjected them, he was himself capable of the fullest response, though it might call upon him to render the supreme sacrifice. He saw subsequent service in England and again in France, where he was assigned to the Motor Transport Division.

He returned to this country in December, 1918, and was mustered out on January 10th, 1919. He again entered the service as Colonel in the Quarter Master Officer's Reserve Corps, in 1921.

On his return from France, he once more took up his railroading pursuits. His latest work, on which he was engaged at the time of his death, was as special assistant to E. J. Pierson, President of the New York, New Haven & Hartford Railroad. He died on Sunday, February 13th, at the Roosevelt Hospital, New York, as the result of an abdominal operation.
