There are few men who have graduated at the Military Academy whose careers have been more varied, efficient, and picturesque than was the career of Charles Hine. The above is the recital of the principal events of his life-those things which all the world who are interested may know. That side of his life which was revealed to his intimates remains to be treated inadequately and briefly, but lovingly.

The dominating elements of Charley Hine's character, and those which drew men to him, were his faithfulness and loyalty,-faithfulness to the truth and to life as he was given to know it, and loyalty to all that comes out of life. His honesty was transparent, and his sense of duty pointed his path of action as the compass points to the pole. It was as impossible for him to compromise with his sense of right as it would be for the waters of the Hudson to run North. His love for those whom he did love knew no bounds, and while he was not demonstrative in his affections, those whom he loved knew it.

His reverance and affection for the Military Academy were as substantial and beautiful as the hills which surround it, and formed an actuating principle in his life. Country, Honor, Duty were to him guiding lights that pointed the pathway in all that he did. It was incomprehensible to him that a son of the Academy could so far forget his Alma Mater as to be guilty of any action that would bring discredit upon her. I am firmly convinced that never from the day of his graduation to that of his death was he cognizant of any act on his part which, if all the world knew it, could have reflected discredit upon her.

To the men of the class of '91, his whole heart went out. It was a positive and genuine affection. He had his favorites among them naturally, but his outstanding friendships were found among the ranks of his classmates. To the rapidly thinning ranks of '91, the vacancy made by Charley Hine's departure can never be closed up, and yet the memory we have of him and the love we bore him will enshrine his place among us as long as there are any left to hold reunions.

Hine was not a member of any religious organization, but his scheme of life was founded on the Ten Commandments and the Golden Rule. He lived his religion every day and hour. Somewhere in the ranks of those who serve and worship Him that sitteth on the Great White Throne, the spirit of Charley Hine is finding happy and useful occupation, and as the zero hour comes for the men of '91, and each goes over the top into the No Man's Land of the spirit world, each will find Charley Hine's loving smile and cheery greeting waiting to make him welcome in the new environment.

Peace to his soul, and may eternal light rest upon him.
author: M. F. D.

obit_text:
On March 1st, 1927, there died in the Tripler General Hospital, Honolulu, while on leave from his duties as Commanding General, 16th Infantry Brigade, with headquarters at Ford Howard, Maryland, Brigadier General LaRoy S. Upton. Although General Upton had not been in very robust health during a year or two previous to his death, his passing away was unexpected and came as a great shock to his family, comrades and hosts of friends.

LaRoy Sunderland Upton was born in Decatur, Michigan, October 8, 1869. He was a son of Captain John Beau and Julia Sherman Upton, both natives of Batavia, New York. They migrated to Michigan shortly after the close of the Civil War. The father of General Upton was a lawyer and a Captain in the infantry of Michigan. He finally settled in Big Rapids, Michigan, where the childhood of General Upton and his nine brothers and sisters was spent. The famous Major General Emory Upton of the Class of 1861, was an uncle of the subject of this sketch. LaRoy attended the common and high schools of his home town and in 1887 spent a few weeks in preparation for West Point at the Institute of the late Senator Ferris.

LaRoy Sunderland Upton reported at the United States Military Academy June 16, 1887, and successfully passed his entrance examinations. On June 12, 1891, he graduated and received a commission as Second Lieutenant, 21st Infantry. He became First Lieutenant of Infantry, February 1, 1898; Captain, September 15, 1900; Major, March 2, 1912; Lieutenant-Colonel, March 2, 1917; Colonel (temporary) August 5, 1917; Brigadier-General, National Army, August 8, 1918; Brigadier-General, Regular Army, November 5, 1923.

General Upton saw the usual peace-time service in many parts of the United States and its foreign possessions. During the Spanish War he was kept on recruiting and other duty in the United States, joining his regiment in Cuba in 1899 where, until 1902, he performed various civilian duties, such as collector of customs, in addition to those of a military nature.

In 1902 he married Miss Agnes M. Wilkinson of Niagara Falls, N. Y. There is one surviving son by this union, Ralph Millae Upton. General Upton remained in the United States until 1906, when he went for duty in the Philippine Islands, returning therefrom in 1908. He went to Panama in 1912, became a student in the Line School, Fort Leavenworth in 1913 and an honor graduate of the Army Staff College in 1915. He was in command of the 30th Infantry 1916-1917 on the Mexican Border. He started for France June 14, 1917 and served in the A. E. F. with the 16th Infantry until July 21, 1917, when he was made Director of Specialties of the First Division School; on October 15, 1917 he organized the First Corps School and remained in command of it until March 3, 1918 when he was assigned to the 9th Infantry stationed in the Rouveois Sector, near St. Mihiel. As Colonel of the 9th Infantry he fought at Chateau Thierry, May 31 to July 9, 1918 and captured the town of Vaux. He participated in the attack east of Villiers-Cotterets Woods July 18-19, 1918, and continued in command of the 9th Infantry until September 7, 1918, when he was assigned to command the 57th Infantry Brigade. As commanding officer of this Brigade, he participated in various Argonne Offensives, October 8-30, 1918. General Upton returned to the United States May 14, 1919 and was detailed a student of General Staff College from which he graduated in July 1920. He then returned to the command of the 9th Infantry. In May 1922 he was appointed Chief of Staff, 7th Corps Area. Promoted to Brigadier-General, Regular Army, in November 1923, he was assigned to command the 16th Infantry Brigade with station at Fort Howard, Maryland. He was on leave of absence when the fatal illness was contracted.

Brigadier-General LaRoy Sunderland Upton was awarded by his own Government the Distinugished Service Cross,

"for extraordinary heroism in action near Soissons, France, July 18 to 19, 1918. His regiment having suffered heavy casualties in its first attack on July 18, 1918, and he having received orders to attack a second time, Colonel Upton re-formed his command and conducted the second attack in person, until stopped by darkness. His line being broken by a gap in its center, all of his battalion commanders being killed or wounded and all of his reserves being in the thinly held line, he established his command post on the extreme front at the right of the gap and remained there for twenty-four hours under steady and intense artillery bombardment and machine-gun fire, holding his position until his regiment was relieved. His presence and his example of fearlessness inspired his weakened line thus to guard the unprotected flank of the whole advance and beat off a violent counter-attack".

The United States also conferred upon him the Distinguished Service Medal,

"for exceptionally meritorious and distinguished services. He commanded with conspicuous ability the 9th Infantry in the trench sector south of Verdun and in all its operations before Chateau Thierry. In the campaign north of Verdun, in October, as commander of the 57th Brigade, he exhibited qualities of brilliant leadership, successfully participating in the battles of Molleville Farm, Grand Montague, Etraye, and in those east of the Meuse. At all times he remained near his front lines, personally directing the attacks and serving as a constant inspiration to his men".

The following foreign governments also recognized the distinguished services of General Upton:

France-the Croix de Guerre with two palms; the French Legion of Honor, Officer.
Italy-the Italian War Cross.
England-the British Order of St. Michael and St. George, Companion.

General Upton was always known to his classmates as "Gobbo", and was noted among them for good humor and irrepressible spirits. He always took a leading part in the amusements and entertainments prepared by cadets, such as "Color Line" and Hundredth Night to June". As an incident of this characteristic, during the Class Dinner at West Point in June 1926, that celebrated the 35th Anniversary of the Graduation of 1891, he and another classmate repeated a comical stunt, including a song which they had perpetrated on one of the other guests present while cadets at the Military Academy.

General Upton was only eighteen years of age on entrance to the Military Academy and in after years stated he never learned really how to study until he entered, in 1913, the Line School at Leavenworth. However, he had little difficulty in graduating from the Military Academy and his after services are full of accomplishment that indicated more than average mental ability. In addition, "Gobbo" was always respected by his classmates and intimates for his high and forceful character. That the traits of honesty, straight-forwardness, industry and loyalty he early displayed continued to develop after graduating from the Military Academy, is evidenced by his career. The Adjutant of the 9th Infantry officially recorded his impressions of the importance of General Upton's services with that regiment during the World War. In it, he states in part as follows:-

"The regiment (9th Infantry) was in dire need of a trained commander with marked force and spirit and one with bull-dog tenacity of purpose who would bring his regiment together and train it as a fighting unit * *

These qualities you brought to the regiment and, by your enthusiasm and zealous efforts communicated a new spirit to the command, which was rapidly losing its fighting morale. The effect of the manner in which you took hold was almost electrical; the morale gradually improved and was brought to a high state. The better officers began to realize the magnitude of the task before them and went about it with renewed effort while the incompetent ones were weeded out. You developed initiative in the command and above all, inspired confidence by your ability to handle any situation that arose and, moreover, you demanded from your subordinates results and you got results.

Due to this it was with an offensive spirit that the regiment entered the trenches in March 1918. When the battalions were under fire, your daily visits to each unit cheered them in their long watch and lessened tension from the anxiety of the new experiences under fire. * *

To you more than to anyone else is due the credit for guiding this regiment and helping it to find itself and establish its character. It was only a short time later in May, 1918, that the terrific onslaught of the Germans broke the 6th French Army in the area south of Soissons and the 2nd Division was rushed in motor camions from the vicinity of Amiens to the demoralized French area. The Ninth was unloaded near Lisy-a-Ourcq at a point on the right flank of the advancing lines of the rapidly moving German units. It was in this precarious situation that you collected the units of your regiment and led them southwestwardly in a 40 kilometer march during the night of May 30, 1918, to a position in front of and across the lines of enemy advance south of the Paris road near the village of Vaux. The holding of this position turned the attack eastward, where it was effectively stopped a few days later. This signal success heartened the demoralized French units in the region, showed the stamina of the 2nd Division, and inspired the confidence of the French high command in the fighting ability of the American troops. Subsequently during the 40 days that the regiment kept contact with the enemy under almost continuous shell fire in its position southeast of the Chateau Thierry, by your daily visits to the units of the front line you kept up a high state of morale during an unusually tense period. Soldiers holding exposed positions of the front line know you by these personal visits and had the utmost confidence in you. It is interesting to note that these visits were in striking contrast to conditions existing in some other units during the same tense period.

It was to your knowledge of the location of your front line and that of the enemy together with the strong fire defense points of the enemy line obtained by personal observations both day and night under shell and machine gun fire, that enabled you in the conference on the plan of attack to stand out so strongly for a direct attack in the assault on Vaux and Bois des Clerembaux, July 1, 1918, which attack proved so eminently successful and so strikingly preferable to the prepared division plans which would have exposed our left flank to enfilade fire from strong enemy machine-gun nests and which would have undoubtedly resulted disastrously for us.

The success of this attack added new hope to the allied cause and paved the way for further and extended advances. In the capture of the strong point of Vaux with its great number of prisoners, the regiment had again shown its mettle. It was only several days after the new line was stabilized that you led the regiment to its attack position on the Bois de Villers-Cotteretts and directed the attack on Vierzy before Soissons on July 18th-19th which broke the enemy lines and entailed the capture of hundreds of enemy prisoners and much materiel.

In all these engagements the morale that you had built up in the 9th Infantry was due to your well founded military knowledge, force, good judgment and personal courage and made the regiment an outstanding one in the whole A. E. F. It was one of the few regiments that always attained its objective and one that never gave ground to the enemy".

It is possible to write indefinitely of the career of General LaRoy Sunderland Upton and the affectionate esteem in which he was held by classmates, friends and comrades. He had the admiration and respect of his subordinates as well as his superiors. The following article is a striking illustration of this fact:-

"Members of the Second Division Association and particularly those who served with the 9th Infantry will regret the death of Brigadier-General LaRoy S. Upton. General Upton joined the 9th Infantry in France as a colonel and with marked force of spirit and tenacity of purpose brought the Regiment to a high degree of efficiency and fused it into the masterful fighting machine that entered the trenches in March 1918. It was only a short time after this the Germans launched their final terrific onslaught against the 6th French Army in the area about Chateau Thierry and the 9th Infantry, as a part of the 2nd Division was rushed by camions to the demoralized French area on the right flank of the rapidly approaching German units. In this precarious position General Upton collected the units of the 9th Infantry and led them in a forty kilometer march during the night of May 30, 1918, to a position near the village of Vaux, where they succeeded in turning eastward the German attack-heartening the demoralized French units-and proving the superb fighting qualities of the 2nd Division.

Through the hideous forty days, under constant shell fire, that the Regiment kept contact with the Germans at Chateau Thierry, General Upton kept the fighting spirit in his men by daily visits to the units in the front lines. Soldiers holding exposed positions in the front lines knew him by these personal visits and gave him their utmost confidence and regard.

The Second Division Association and the Regular Army join in saluting the passing of an officer whose loss will be gravely felt by both of these organizations".

There is no more fitting way of closing this brief sketch of the life and accomplishments of this gallant and distinguished graduate of the U. S. Military Academy than by quoting the official French opinion of him, written in the Citation awarding Brigadier-General LaRoy Sunderland Upton the Croix de Guerre, with Palm:

"A general officer of unflagging energy and an absolute contempt for danger; during the operations to the North of Verdun in October 1918, afforded his troops the finest example of boldness and courage, shattering the resistance of the enemy and contributing greatly to the success of the operation".
author: PALMER E. PIERCE
